variables.content:
For a heavy-fermion metal tuned by an external magnetic field $B$ to a field-tuned quantum critical point at $B=B_{c0}$, the low-temperature Landau quasiparticle effective mass $M^{*}(B)$ in the restored Landau-Fermi-liquid regime for $B>B_{c0}$ exhibits power-law scaling with reduced field,
      $$
        M^{*}(B)\propto (B-B_{c0})^{\alpha},
      $$
      with critical exponent $\alpha=-1/2$; here $B_{c0}$ denotes the critical magnetic field that tunes the system to the quantum critical point and $M^{*}(B)$ is the quasiparticle effective mass relevant to low-temperature thermodynamic and transport properties.
      [1]
      [12]

Inclusion of a static magnetic field $H$ introduces the dimensionless ratio $R=\mu_B H/T$, where $\mu_B$ is the Bohr magneton and $T$ the temperature, and modifies the critical effective-mass scaling at the quantum critical density $\rho_\infty$ to
      $$
      \frac{M}{M^*(T,H,\rho_\infty)}\propto T^{2/3}\,a(R),
      $$
      with $a(R)$ a scaling function of $R$. In the zero-temperature limit $T\to0$ (equivalently $R\to\infty$) this scaling yields the analytic field law
      $$
      M^*(T=0,H,\rho_\infty)\propto H^{2/3}.
      $$
      Therefore, application of a static magnetic field satisfying $\mu_B H>T$ renders the effective mass $M^*$ finite and restores conventional Landau Fermi-liquid behavior. Numerical calculations based on this field-dependent scaling reproduce the experimentally observed scaling of magnetization and susceptibility for the heavy-fermion metal CeRu$_2$Si$_2$ and of the ratio $C/T$ for the heavy-fermion compound YbRh(Si_{0.95}Ge_{0.05})_2 over ranges of small applied fields.

The coincident $T^{-1/2}$ power laws observed in the Sommerfeld coefficient $C/T$, the uniform susceptibility $\chi$, and the normalized thermal expansion $\alpha/T$, together with the universal scaling of the normalized effective mass across different heavy-fermion materials, are consistently accounted for within a quasiparticle framework in which the electronic system undergoes a fermion-condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT); under this interpretation, the non-Fermi-liquid (NFL) thermodynamic behavior observed at quantum critical points of these heavy-fermion metals is attributed to FCQPT-driven renormalization of the quasiparticle mass rather than to material-specific critical fluctuation modes, so that multiple different fluctuation-dominated quantum critical points hypothesized for different compounds can be conceptually replaced by a single FCQPT-related quantum critical point that controls the observed universal NFL thermodynamics.

The derivation of the T^{-2/3} temperature dependence of the effective mass M*(T) for ZnCu3(OH)6Cl2 assumes that the low-energy degrees of freedom of the S=1/2 kagome antiferromagnet are described by a strongly correlated quantum spin liquid (SCQSL) composed of neutral fermionic spinons that occupy a Fermi sphere with Fermi momentum p_F, have zero electrical charge, and acquire a large, renormalized effective mass M* due to proximity to a fermion-condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT) treated as the quantum critical point; under this assumption the theoretical scaling relations and Landau Fermi-liquid quasiparticle reasoning developed for heavy-fermion electronic systems (where FCQPT gives rise to temperature- and field-dependent M*) are applied directly to the kagome spin system.
      Refs. 9–14 (theoretical FCQPT literature) and Eqs. (1)–(3) in the paper

For a homogeneous heavy-electron liquid near fermion-condensation quantum criticality, in elevated-temperature regimes above the low-temperature crossover scale $T^{*}(B)$ the quasiparticle effective mass exhibits two distinct asymptotic power laws: (i) in the intermediate regime with temperatures of order the crossover scale, $T\sim T^{*}(B)$, one has $M^{*}(T)\propto T^{-2/3}$; and (ii) in a higher-temperature regime $T>T^{*}(B)$ one has $M^{*}(T)\propto 1/\sqrt{T}$. These two specific power laws are asserted as the asymptotic temperature dependences in their respective regimes for the effective mass controlling thermodynamics and transport.

The crossover between non-Fermi-liquid and Fermi-liquid regimes is governed by a field-induced Fermi energy set by the Zeeman energy of the critical modes, k_{B}T_{F} ∼ gμ_{B}B, so that the characteristic Fermi temperature T_{F} grows linearly with applied field B; furthermore, for the empirically determined scaling exponent α = 3/2 the quasiparticle effective mass m^{*}(B), identified thermodynamically with the zero-temperature specific-heat coefficient, scales as m^{*}(B) ∝ B^{\alpha-2} ∝ B^{-1/2}.

For a heavy-fermion metal tuned near a topological fermion-condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT), define the quasiparticle effective mass $M^{*}(T)$ as the temperature-dependent mass at $B\approx B_{c0}$ and $M^{*}(B)$ as the low-temperature field-dependent mass at $T\to0$; the dominant asymptotic dependences in the experimentally relevant non-Fermi-liquid and field-tuned Landau-Fermi-liquid regimes are
      $$
      M^{*}(T)\simeq a_T\,T^{-2/3}\quad\text{(for }B\approx B_{c0}\text{)}
      $$
      and
      $$
      M^{*}(B)\simeq a_B\,(B-B_{c0})^{-2/3}\quad\text{(for }T\to0,\ B>B_{c0}\text{)},
      $$
      with positive prefactors $a_T,a_B$ and critical field $B_{c0}$ (for CeCoIn$_5$, $B_{c0}\simeq5.0\ \mathrm{T}$); these $-2/3$ exponents describe the effective-mass scaling in the temperature and field windows used to fit experimental data on CeCoIn$_5$.

At least one quantum phase transition of the fermion-condensation type (FCQPT) exists inside or at the edge of the superconducting dome of high-temperature superconductors (HTSC); this FCQPT constitutes a common quantum critical point (QCP) that governs the field-induced restoration of Landau Fermi-liquid behavior in both HTSC and heavy-fermion metals, and it accounts for (a) the magnetic-field dependence of the quasiparticle effective mass and of transport prefactors, (b) the approximately linear-in-field crossover temperature T^{*}(B), and (c) the universal normalized effective-mass scaling; for Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CuO_{6+x} the FCQPT critical field inferred from the crossover line is B_{c0}\approx 6\ \mathrm{T}, placing at least one FCQPT inside or at the boundary of the superconducting region.

For a homogeneous fermion system tuned to the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT) quantum critical point, the temperature-dependent Landau integral equation
      $$
      \frac{1}{M^{*}(T)}=\frac{1}{M^{*}}+\int \frac{\mathbf{p}_{F}\cdot\mathbf{p}_{1}}{p_{F}^{3}}\,F(\mathbf{p}_{F},\mathbf{p}_{1})\;\frac{\partial n_{1}(p_{1},T)}{\partial p_{1}}\frac{d\mathbf{p}_{1}}{(2\pi)^{3}},
      $$
      becomes homogeneous because the zero-temperature reciprocal effective mass $1/M^{*}$ (with $M^{*}$ the $T=0$ renormalized quasiparticle effective mass) can be neglected when $M^{*}\to\infty$ at the quantum critical point; here $\mathbf{p}_{F}$ is the Fermi momentum vector, $p_F=|\mathbf{p}_F|$, $F(\mathbf{p}_{F},\mathbf{p}_{1})$ is the Landau interaction amplitude, and $n_{1}(p,T)=n(p,T)-n(p)$ is the temperature-induced deviation of the quasiparticle occupation $n(p,T)$ from the $T=0$ step function $n(p)$. Solving this homogeneous integral equation in the regime where the momentum-integral term controls the temperature dependence yields the non-Fermi-liquid asymptotic scaling of the thermodynamic quasiparticle effective mass
      $$
      M^{*}(T)\propto T^{-2/3},
      $$
      valid in the temperature range where the integral term in the Landau equation dominates and $M^{*}$ is the thermodynamic effective mass that governs quantities such as the specific-heat coefficient $C/T$ and the magnetic susceptibility $\chi$.

For a fermionic system that has undergone FCQPT and whose FC order parameter is suppressed by an applied magnetic field B > B_c, the asserted field dependence of the quasiparticle effective mass in the restored Landau Fermi liquid is M^*(B) ∝ 1/√B, where the proportionality constant depends on microscopic details; this scaling has been argued theoretically and reported in selected experimental studies of heavy-electron compounds but is not claimed as a universal law for all systems with FC.
      [20,21]
